coredata.dc:title: Photo-transformation of graphitic carbon nitride synthesized from different precursors: Influence on environmental fate and photocatalytic water disinfection
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Shi’en
Wang, Wanjun
Wang, Chao
Li, Weixuan
Xu, Zhe
Li, Guiying
An, Taicheng
coredata.dc:description: Photo-stability and environmental impacts of photocatalysts are critical issues for large-scale applications. Herein, photo-transformation properties of graphitic carbon nitride (CNs) as water disinfection photocatalysts synthesized from precursors of urea (UCN), thiourea (TCN) and melamine (MCN) were studied for the first time. Results demonstrated that all the CNs were unstable under photoaging, with photo-stability following the order of TCN < UCN < MCN. This was associated to the highest H2O2 production ability of TCN, indicating the non-radical species were also important to determine the photo-stability of CNs. Photocatalytic disinfection performances were in the order of UCN > TCN > MCN, which was due to the differences in specific surface area and band structures. The disinfection activities were all decreased after photoaging, attributing to the partial decomposition of CNs. In addition, bacterial inactivation efficiencies of CNs aqueous filtrates (CN-FTs) after photoaging was firstly studied. Interestingly, it was unexpected to find that the CN-FTs possessed considerable photocatalytic disinfection activities with TCN-FT being the highest, probably due to the release of carbon dots-like substances. The water stability of TCN was also the highest due to its most negative Zeta potential leading to the lowest sedimentation rate, which indicates TCN and its photo-transformation products might cause persistent adverse environmental effects when releasing into water bodies. This work highlighted that CNs from different precursors have distinct influence on photocatalytic water disinfection activity and environmental fate, which should be concerned for their real applications.

(a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CNs and aged CNs; (b) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) patterns of CNs and aged CNs; High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) N 1s spectra of (c) UCN and aged UCN, (d) TCN and aged TCN, and (e) MCN and aged MCN.

Dash line indicates the model fitting curves.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-stability and environmental impacts of photocatalysts are critical issues for large-scale applications. Herein, photo-transformation properties of graphitic carbon nitride (CNs) as water disinfection photocatalysts synthesized from precursors of urea (UCN), thiourea (TCN) and melamine (MCN) were studied for the first time. Results demonstrated that all the CNs were unstable under photoaging, with photo-stability following the order of TCN < UCN < MCN. This was associated to the highest H2O2 production ability of TCN, indicating the non-radical species were also important to determine the photo-stability of CNs. Photocatalytic disinfection performances were in the order of UCN > TCN > MCN, which was due to the differences in specific surface area and band structures. The disinfection activities were all decreased after photoaging, attributing to the partial decomposition of CNs. In addition, bacterial inactivation efficiencies of CNs aqueous filtrates (CN-FTs) after photoaging was firstly studied. Interestingly, it was unexpected to find that the CN-FTs possessed considerable photocatalytic disinfection activities with TCN-FT being the highest, probably due to the release of carbon dots-like substances. The water stability of TCN was also the highest due to its most negative Zeta potential leading to the lowest sedimentation rate, which indicates TCN and its photo-transformation products might cause persistent adverse environmental effects when releasing into water bodies. This work highlighted that CNs from different precursors have distinct influence on photocatalytic water disinfection activity and environmental fate, which should be concerned for their real applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of economy and society, water pollution has been recognized as a serious problem that cannot be ignored. Polluted water resources have become a hotbed for the spread of multiple pathogens [1–3]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop clean, efficient and stable water disinfection methods. However, traditional disinfection methods such as chlorination, ozonation, and UV disinfection have the disadvantages of high energy consumption, toxic disinfection by-products and safety problems [4]. Photocatalytic disinfection method has the advantages of cleanliness and energy saving, since it can use solar light as the energy source without secondary pollution. It has been received extensive attention in the field of wastewater treatment as well as water disinfection [5]. Therefore, much effort has been devoted to the research of finding cheap and stable photocatalysts that can effectively inactivate water pathogens.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, denoted as CNs thereafter) is an emerging photocatalyst, which has been received considerable attention because of its outstanding features, including intrinsic 2D crystalline nature, excellent visible-light responsivity, facile synthesis and low costs [6]. CNs has been broadly used in various applications including water splitting [7], molecule functionalization [8], organic compounds synthesis [9], pollutant decomposition [10–12], electrochemical sensing [13], Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production [14]. In particular, there is great potential for photocatalytic inactivation of water pathogens using CNs-based photocatalysts [15–17]. However, most of the current research has only focused on the CNs-based material synthesis, bacterial inactivation efficiencies and kinetics [18,19]. Although concrete progress has been achieved, the photo-stability and photo-transformation products of CNs have not been fully revealed [20]. In addition, the environmental fate of engineering nanoparticles after entering natural waters and its effect on the ecological health of water environment have received increasing attention [21,22]. Therefore, as an emerging carbonaceous material to be widely used in water disinfection and many other environmental fields, it is very important to clarify the photo-transformation properties of CNs to reveal the influence on environmental fate and photocatalytic bacterial inactivation efficiencies before large-scale industrial applications.

There have been a number of reports on the photo-stability, transformation mechanisms and environmental fate of carbonaceous materials, such as graphene oxide (GO) [23–25], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [26], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [27]. As an emerging carbonaceous photocatalyst, CNs with graphite-like structure are likely to face similar challenges. Wang et al. [28] has found that the catalytic performance of CNs for the decomposition of diclofenac sodium was reduced after photo-aging due to self-decomposition. The photocatalytic self-decomposition was mainly attributed to the attack of radical species (i.e. ·OH and ·O2 −), leading to cleavage of heptazine unit and producing a variety of decomposed products including cyanuric acid [29,30]. Li et al. [31] has reported that some weaker oxidants (i.e. Cl2·− and ClO·) could also contribute to photo-transformation of CNs. However, none of these studies have systematically studied the physicochemical structural changes of CNs before and after photoaging treatment, and their effects to bacterial inactivation. In addition, CNs can be synthesized by a variety of precursors with different photocatalytic properties [32]. The effects of different precursors on the photo-transformation and the photocatalytic disinfection properties of CNs have not been explored. Furthermore, the effect of possible released intermediates during the photoaging process of CNs on the bacterial inactivation efficiencies is not clear.

CNs have a wide range of sources and can be synthesized from various nitrogen-rich precursors, in which urea, thiourea and melamine are readily available and represent the most widely used precursors [18,33–35]. Therefore, in this study, CNs were synthesized from the three representative precursors (i.e. urea, thiourea and melamine), which were subjected to photoaging process under visible light (VL) irradiation. The changes of physicochemical structures including crystal structure, band structure, functional groups and morphologies of CNs before and after the photo-transformation process were studied in detail. Colloidal stability of CNs affects the sedimentation characteristics of CNs in water bodies, thus significantly influence the environmental fate of CNs after being released into the environment. Therefore, the influence of photo-transformation process on the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation properties as well as the colloidal stability of CNs were assessed to reveal the environmental fate of CNs. Moreover, the possible released intermediates during the photo-transformation process and their effects on the bacterial inactivation efficiencies were also investigated. To date, this is the first study that systematically compared the CNs synthesized from different precursors in terms of physicochemical structural changes, photo-transformation properties and environmental effects after photoaging. The study could provide valuable information on the influence of photoaging on the environmental fate of CNs and provide valuable basis for the real application of CNs for solar-driven photocatalytic water disinfection and many other applications.

Urea (99 %, Macklin), thiourea (99 %, Macklin) and melamine (> 99 %, Aladdin) were used as precursors to synthesize CNs by using thermal polymerization methods according to previous studies [28,36–39]. Briefly, 20 g of urea, melamine or thiourea was placed in a 50 mL lidded porcelain crucible. The temperature was raised to 550 °C in a muffle furnace and maintained for 4 h, then cooled naturally. All the obtained products were fully ground to powder for further experiments and characterization. The CNs synthesized from urea, thiourea and melamine were recorded as UCN, TCN and MCN, respectively. The formation mechanisms of CNs have been established in previous studies [40,41]. Briefly, for MCN, melem was first formed by condensation of melamine, followed by polymerization to produce carbon nitride frameworks. For UCN and TCN, the precursors of urea and thiourea were first polymerized to generate the melamine molecules, and the subsequent reaction paths were identical to that of MCN produced from melamine (Scheme S1).

Photo-transformation experiments were performed in a multi-channel photochemical reactor (Perfectlight PCX50C, China) equipped with white LED as the VL source. Briefly, 50 mg of CNs samples (UCN, TCN or MCN) were dispersed in 50 mL ultrapure water in the reactor under magnetic stirring at 150 r/min, which were then irradiated with the LED light for 6 h. The light intensity was determined to be 352 mW/cm2 by a light meter (Perfectlight PL-MW200, China). The selection of light intensity and irradiation time was based on pre-experiments that the CNs can achieve a good photocatalytic disinfection efficiency. After the photo-aging process, the suspension was filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane. Both the precipitates and filtrates were collected and denoted as aged CNs (aged UCN, aged TCN, aged MCN) and CNs filtrates (UCN-FT, TCN-FT, MCN-FT), respectively. Particle sizes of the CNs samples before and after photoaging process were analyzed by a laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Mastersizer 3000, UK). The measurements were conducted in triplicates.

The samples were characterized by multiple technologies, including particle sizes determination, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS), and Mott-Shottky analysis. The details can be found in Supporting Information (Text S1).

Total organic carbon of (TOC) in filtrate of CNs samples was tested by a total organic carbon analyzer (Analytik Jena AG, multiN/C 3100, German). The generation of radicals was tested by electron spin resonance (ESR) technology using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) (for ·OH and ·O2 −) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) (for 1O2) as trapping agents [42] (Text S2). The produced H2O2 was determined by DPD/POD method [43] (Text S3). The photo-transformation products of the g-C3N4 samples exposed to visible light irradiation were determined by electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry (ESI MS, Bruker MicroTOF-Q, German). The ESI interface was operated in negative pressure mode, and the parameters were set as follows: capillary voltage 3500 V, nebulizer gas (N2) pressure 5.0 bar, drying heater temperature 180 °C, drying gas (N2) flow 6.0 L min −1, collision energy 5.0 V. Full scan spectra were obtained from 50 to 2000 by m/z scanning [30]. Zeta potential was measured by a dynamic laser scattering analyzer (DLS, Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK). All the experiments were conducted in triplicates.

Photocatalytic bacterial inactivation experiments were conducted using E. coli as the representative bacteria. Briefly, E. coli suspension (107 CFU/mL) and g-C3N4 samples before and after the photoaging process were put into the multi-channel photochemical reactor (Perfectlight PCX50C, China). After illuminating for 6 h, aliquots of the suspension were collected, and the viable cell concentrations were determined by plate count methods [44]. More details can be found in Text S4.The bacterial inactivation rate constant was calculated using the Log-Linear + Shoulder model [45,46], details can be found in Text S5. The experiments were conducted in triplicates.

To study the water stability of CNs, sedimentation kinetics were analyzed by monitoring the absorbance of each suspension for 60 min at a wavelength of 320 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (RAYLEIGH UV-9600, China). This test could provide insights into the sedimentation characteristics of CNs in water, so as to partially reveal their environmental fate. Briefly, suspensions of CNs and aged CNs (1 g/L) were prepared, and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 by 0.1 M H2SO4 and NaOH solution. The suspensions were fully shaken before sedimentation tests. The absorbance of the suspension was determined at 0, 2.5, 14, 20 and 60 min, and 2 parallel groups were set up for each sample. The sedimentation rate constant (k) was obtained by exponential function fitting (At = A0e-kt) using Excel 2016, where A0 is the absorbance at time zero and At is the absorbance at time t.

SPSS 25.0 (PASW Statistics, IBM Company, USA) was used for sample independence t-test and Levin's variance equivalence test. p < 0.05 indicates statistical difference, p < 0.01 indicates significant statistical difference, and p < 0.001 suggests very significant statistical difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-stability of the CNs during photoaging process
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the chemical stability of CNs synthesized from different precursors during VL photoaging process, particle size distribution of CNs before and after irradiation were tested. It was found the proportion of large particles was decreased with the increase of small particles after 6 h of VL irradiation (Fig. S1). The median diameter (D50) of MCN decreased significantly from 29.57 μm to 11.77 μm (p < 0.05). In addition, the D50 particle size of UCN decreased significantly from 4.96 μm to 1.36 μm (p < 0.001), while the D50 particle size of TCN also decreased significantly from 3.67 μm to 3.33 μm (p < 0.001) (Fig. 1(a)). This result indicated that the CNs were essentially unstable under VL-induced photoaging, which results in the decease of particle sizes. The released TOC were further monitored. No TOC release was detected under dark condition. With light irradiation, it was found that released TOC in the filtered solution was 1.52, 7.36 and 0.44 mg/L for UCN, TCN and MCN, respectively (Fig. 1(b)), confirming that all the CNs would undergo photo-transformation process with the release of small organic molecules. In addition, the photo-stability of the CNs followed the order of TCN < UCN < MCN, based on the released TOC contents during the photoaging process. During the process of photo-transform, the structures of CNs were damaged, which led to the decomposition of CNs to produce small fragments, and some dissolved intermediates were released into the water. Therefore, the particle sizes of CNs were decreased with the increase of TOC in the solution.

XRD was used to characterize the crystal structural changes of the CNs after photoaging (Fig. 2(a)). The (100) peak at 12.92° ∼ 13.02° corresponds to the distance between the centers of heptazine ring as in-plane repeating unit of g-C3N4 [47–49]. Compared with MCN, the (100) peaks of TCN and UCN were weaker, indicating that their in-plane structures were less ordered. This was because that the precursors of thiourea and urea released more gases during synthesis process, which disturbed the graphite-like structures and reduced the order of in-plane repeating units [42,50]. This might lead to the lower photo-stability of TCN/UCN than that of MCN. The (002) peak at 27.49° ∼ 27.73° is derived from the interlayer reflection of the graphite-like structure [47,49,51,52]. The d spacing of stacking layers of UCN was the lowest among the CNs (0.321 nm) (Table S1), suggesting it would have the highest lateral charge transport and interlayer exciton dissociation efficiency [53], which can lead to the highest photocatalytic activity of UCN (as confirmed in Fig. 6). After photoaging, the XRD peak profiles of the CNs did not change significantly, indicating that the photo-transformation did not cause obvious crystal structural changes of the CNs.

FT-IR was further used to study the changes of functional groups (Fig. 2(b)). For all the CNs, the broad peak around 3000– 3400 cm−1 is from the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH, and the peak around 810 cm−1 is from the breathing mode of tri-s-triazine unit [50,54]. Compared with UCN and MCN, unique peaks of TCN appeared at 2165– 2180 cm−1, which could be attributed to terminal cyano group (CN or N=C=N) [55,56] that are resulted from the cutting of s-heptazine skeleton by NH3 and H2S gases during the synthesis of TCN [57]. For UCN and MCN, no obvious changes of functional groups were found after photoaging, while for TCN, a new peak near 888 cm−1 occurred, which can be assigned to the incompletely condensed amino groups [33]. This result indicates that more terminal amino groups were formed on TCN, which may lead to its lowest photo-stability. Raman spectra of CNs and aged CNs were shown in Fig. S2(a), with characteristic peaks located at 471, 707, 770, 1232, and 1310 cm−1, further confirming the presence of graphitic carbon nitride structures [58]. The peak signals of TCN and MCN were relatively weak, which might be due to fluorescence interference [59]. There were no significant changes in peak positions of Raman shifts for the CNs before and after photoaging. XPS survey spectrum showed that all the samples were mainly composed of C, N and O without other impurities (Fig. S2(b)). In particular, high-resolution S 2p spectrum showed that no obvious S element was detected in TCN (Fig. S2(c)), although TCN was synthesized by thiourea (a sulfur-containing precursor). This can be explained by that the sulfur species were easy to be released in the form of H2S and CS2 in the process of thermal polymerization [41]. High-resolution C 1s spectra of CNs and aged CNs can be decomposed into three peaks at about 288.04 eV, 286.20 eV and 292.30 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and N=C-N2 (Fig. S2(d)-(f)). While N 1s spectra can be decomposed into three deconvolution peaks at 399.93 eV, 401.16 eV and 404.58 eV, corresponding to C-N-C, N-(C)3 and C-N-Hx, respectively (Fig. 2(c)-(e), Table S2). The TCN exhibited higher proportion of terminal amino groups (C-N-Hx) than UCN and MCN. These terminal amino groups could act as electron-withdrawing groups, which were more prone to be attacked by ROSs due to the presence of lone pair electrons [29,60,61]. Therefore, compared to other CNs, TCN with a higher proportion of terminal amino groups was more vulnerable to be attacked by ROSs, which led to the lowest photo-stability of TCN. The N species were decreased after photoaging, which was related to the destruction of tri-s-triazine ring structure in CNs. This might lead to a decrease in their photocatalytic activity, thereby reducing their photocatalytic disinfection performances (as confirmed in Fig. 6).The atomic ratio of O in TCN increased from 3 % to 4.18 %, while it remained almost the same in UCN and MCN (Table S2), suggesting TCN was more likely to be oxidized, which was in agreement with its lowest photo-stability among the CNs.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of all the CNs showed type IV isotherms with hysteresis loops (Fig. 3(a)-(c)), suggesting mesoporous structure. As shown in Table S3, UCN has the largest specific surface area (74.20 m2/g) and pore volume (0.43 cm3/g) among the CNs, which was probably due to the released NH3 gases from the decomposition of urea caused the formation of more porous structures of UCN [62]. After photoaging, the specific surface area and pore volume of UCN significantly decreased to 33.92 m2/g and 0.13 m3/g, respectively. TCN and MCN had much smaller specific surface areas and pore volumes than those of UCN. After photoaging, the specific surface area of TCN increased from 4.06 to 16.76 m2/g with increase of pore volume from 0.04 to 0.17 cm3/g, while the specific surface area of MCN decreased slightly from 9.12 to 8.71 m2/g with no changes of pore volume. These differences were due to the different ROSs produced by the UCN, TCN and MCN (Section 3.3). SEM images showed stacked layer structure of the CNs (Fig. 3(d)-(f)). TEM images further demonstrated that the CNs have clear and smooth edges before photoaging (Fig. 3(g)-(i)), while they become broken and irregular after photoaging (Fig. 3(j)-(l)). In particular, the porous structure of UCN was lost after photoaging (Fig. S3), which was in accordance with the results in Table S3 showing that the pore volume and pore size of UCN were decreased after photoaging, suggesting the photocatalytic oxidation can lead to the collapse of the porosity of CNs. The optical properties of CNs and aged CNs were studied by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 4(a)), and the band gap energies (Eg) were calculated using Tauc plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν (Fig. S4(a)-(c)) according to previous study [63]. All the CNs exhibited VL absorption, and the Eg of UCN, TCN and MCN were found to be 2.61, 2.30 and 2.45 eV, respectively. After photoaging, the Eg of CNs remained almost the same, because the photo-transformation did not cause obvious crystal structural changes of the CNs (Fig. 2). The flat band potential (Vfb) was determined by Mott-Schottky measurements to be −0.63 V, −0.80 V, and − 0.65 V vs. Ag/AgCl for UCN, TCN and MCN, respectively (equivalent to −0.40 V, −0.57 V, and − 0.42 vs. NHE, respectively) (Fig. S4(d)-(f)). The conductive band (CB) potentials are approximately 0.1 V lower than the Vfb [64]. Therefore, based on the Equation: Eg = EVB - ECB, the EVB can be determined, and the results were close to VB-XPS results (Fig. S5).The band structures of the CNs were shown in Fig. 4(b). It showed that the ECB of all the CNs were more negative than the redox potential of O2/·O2 −, while only the EVB of UCN was more positive than the redox potential of ·OH/OH−, suggesting that only UCN could produce ·OH at valence band (VB). These produced reactive oxygen species (ROSs) including ·OH might be responsible for the decomposition and transformation of the CNs under photoaging process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ROSs and photo-transformation mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the roles of different ROSs in the photo-transformation process, ESR spin-trap with DMPO/TEMP technique was used to analyze ·OH, ·O2 − and 1O2 radicals [65,66]. The DMPO-·OH signal intensity followed the order of UCN > MCN > TCN (Fig. 5(a)), which was in accordance with the above-mentioned band structures of the CNs. In the case of UCN, the ·OH can be produced from both CB and VB, resulting in its highest ·OH productivity. DMPO-·O2 − and TEMP-1O2 signals were detected in all the CNs samples (Fig. 5(b)-(c)). The 1O2 was believed to be produced by the h+ oxidation of ·O2 − [20]. H2O2 production efficiency was also monitored, which showed the order of TCN > UCN > MCN (Fig. 5(d)). This is because that H2O2 was believed to be mainly produced by O2 reduction via photo-generated e− trapping at the CB [67]. A more negative CB potential suggests a higher O2 reduction activity to produce H2O2. Therefore, given that the ECB of TCN was more negative than that of UCN and MCN (Fig. 4(b)), the H2O2 production activity of TCN was the highest among the CNs. The produced H2O2 might play the most important role to cause the decomposition of TCN when exposing to photoaging treatment. This might be due to the much higher lifetime of H2O2 than ·OH and ·O2 −. For instance, it has been reported that ·OH radical could instantaneously quench in 4 × 10−9 s, while the half-life of H2O2 could last for several days [68]. Previous studies have proposed that the ·OH and ·O2 − were the major ROSs that led to the decomposition of CNs [29,30,69]. In this study, we additionally proposed that the major ROSs were quite different when using different precursors to synthesize CNs, and non-radical species (i.e. H2O2) might also play roles in determining the photo-stability of CNs.

Furthermore, the photo-transformation intermediates of TCN were identified by ESI MS spectral analysis, and the possible molecular structures of some intermediates were deduced based on the m/z value (Fig. S6). These intermediates were suspected to be fragments of the tri-s-triazine structure of CNs after varying degrees of oxidative damage, which were basically consistent with the intermediate products of CNs under UV irradiation [29]. It has been found that the oxygen-containing groups including O=C–O, CO, NO and N–C–O could be generated on CNs surface under H2O2 treatment [70]. To confirm whether H2O2 has the ability to oxidize CNs, additional experiments were conducted by exposing the CNs to H2O2 treatment. FT-IR and XPS were used to characterize the H2O2-treated CNs (H-CNs). In the FT-IR spectra of H-CNs (Fig. S7(a)), the absorption peaks observed between 1000 and 1750 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of heptazine rings [55,71], indicating that H-CNs retained the carbon nitride structure after H2O2 treatment. XPS results further revealed significant changes in the elemental composition of H-CNs compared to CNs, with a notable increase in the proportion of O (Fig. S7(b), Table S4). The XPS O1s spectra suggested that CO and CO functionalities were the predominant oxygen-containing groups in H-CNs (Fig. S7(c)-(e)). These results indicated that H2O2 was indeed capable of oxidizing CNs. Therefore, it is proposed that H2O2 attacked electron-rich groups on CNs such as amino groups and hydroxyl groups, leading to the formation of ketone groups, followed by ring-opening reactions with the aid of ·OH and ·O2 −. Photo-induced holes (h+) could also directly oxidize and contribute to the ring-opening reactions [28]. Carboxyl groups were then generated and removed with the release of CO2, as supported by the mineralization of CNs (Fig. 1(b)). The possible photo-transformation pathways were shown in Fig. 5(e). Through the circular reactions, the CNs was gradually decomposed and mineralized with the reduce of particle sizes. It should be noted that the aforementioned pathway was one potential photo-transformation pathway of CNs, inferred from the decomposition products of TCN. Further studies were still needed to fully clarified the decomposition pathways of various CNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic disinfection performance of fresh/aged CNs and CNs filtrates
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore how the photoaging process can affect the photocatalytic disinfection performance of the CNs, so as to shed light on the robustness of CNs in real disinfection system and the possible ecological impacts if they were released into the environment after treatment, we conducted the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation tests using the fresh and aged CNs. No bacterial (E. coli) inactivation was found under dark control experiments (Fig. S8(a)). As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fresh UCN exhibited the best ability among the CNs to inactivate E. coli, which could reduce ∼4.5 log of viable cells within 6 h. In contrast, only ∼0.8 and ∼ 1.3 log of viable E. coli cells were inactivated in the case of fresh TCN, and MCN, respectively. UCN exhibited the highest photocatalytic inactivation performance, which was comparable to CNs reported in previous studies (Table S5). The high photocatalytic disinfection performance of UCN was due to the large surface area, high porosity (Table S3), as well as the optimal band structure of UCN to produce multiple kinds of ROSs (Fig. 5). After photoaging, the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation efficiencies over the aged CNs also followed the order of aged UCN > aged TCN > aged MCN, but the efficiencies were all deceased compared with the fresh CNs (Fig. 6(b)). The bacterial inactivation kinetics were then fitted with Log-Linear + Shoulder model, and the specific inactivation rate (kmax) were calculated (Fig. 6(c), Table S6). The kmax of UCN (2.35 h−1) was 2.37 and 2.6 times higher than that of TCN (0.99 h−1) and MCN (0.90 h−1), respectively. However, such enhanced multiples decreased after photoaging. This result suggests that the activity deterioration for UCN was more serious than that of TCN/MCN when exposing to photoaging. This is because that the highest photocatalytic disinfection activity of UCN was mainly attributed to its largest specific surface area and porosity (Table S3). This porous structure was fragile (Fig. S3), and even minor changes in UCN's porous structure would lead to significant deterioration of disinfection activities.

Previous studies have explored the photocatalytic activity changes of g-C3N4-based materials by recycling experiments, and most of them achieved a good recyclability [72,73]. Recently, more and more studies have found that g-C3N4 might undergo photo-decomposition process, and pointed that its environmental fate and impact to ecological systems should be taken consideration [30,69]. However, few of them have explored the environmental effects of released photo-transformation products. Herein, the CNs filtrates after the photoaging were collected and tested for their antibacterial activity under VL irradiation. No bacterial inactivation was found under dark conditions for the CNs filtrates (Fig. S8(b)). However, we unexpectedly found that the CNs filtrates possessed different photocatalytic bacterial inactivation activities based on different precursors. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the filtrates from TCN (TCN-FT) showed the highest activity, which could inactivate ∼4 log of E. coli cells within 6 h of irradiation, with kmax reaching 2.78 h−1. The MCN-FT had weak disinfection ability, and only ∼0.7 log of cells was inactivated within 6 h (kmax = 1.37 h−1), while UCN-FT showed almost no photocatalytic disinfection activity.

To explain this, the released substances in the filtrates were visually observed by TEM. It was found that large amounts of tiny nanoparticles (similar to carbon quantum dots), were observed in the CNs filtrates (Fig. S9(a)-(c)). The concentration of these tiny nanoparticles in TCN-FT were much higher than that of MCN-FT and UCN-FT, which matched well with the above results that the released TOC from TCN was the highest (Fig. 1(b)). As shown in Fig. S9(d), FT-IR analysis further proved that the characteristic peak of triazine unit of g-C3N4 near 810 cm−1 were not observed, while strong peak appeared at 1630 cm−1 corresponding to NH2 angular vibration [28], suggesting the basic structure of g-C3N4 was destructed and amino functionalized carbon dots were produced and released into the filtrates. Carbon dots have been reported to possess photo-chemical reactivity to generate ROSs [74]. Therefore, it is proposed that these released carbon dots led to the photocatalytic inactivation of bacterial cells. UCN and MCN could produce ·OH, which might cause the further oxidation of released carbon dots, significantly minimizing the amounts of carbon dots. While only non-radical species (i.e. 1O2 and H2O2) were produced on TCN, which led to the lower mineralization ability and more photo-transformation intermediates (i.e. carbon dots) were produced. The released carbon dots-like substances had photo-induced biological toxicity, which should be paid attention if they released to the aquatic environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence on the environmental fate of CNs
ce:section.ce:para: Water stability is a crucial factor to predict the environmental fate and transport of nanoparticles in aquatic environment. To evaluate the water stability and sedimentation properties, Zeta potential of fresh CNs and aged CNs were measured at pH 7.0. The isoelectric point of g-C3N4 materials has been reported to be 4–5 [33], and therefore all the CNs were found to be negatively charged at pH 7.0 (Fig. 7(a)). The fresh UCN exhibited less negative Zeta potential (−28.7 mV) than that of TCN (−36.7 mV) and MCN (−30.7 mV). This was due to the existence of surface amine groups could act as proton acceptors to obtain positive surface charges [33]. The UCN had much higher surface area than TCN and MCN (Table S3), which could enhance the active sites and surface amine groups exposed to the solution, moving the Zeta potential towards positive direction. Therefore, UCN had less negative Zeta potential than TCN and MCN, which might lead to the increased sedimentation rate and decreased water stability, because the electrostatic repulsion between CNs particles would decease due to the less charged surfaces. To confirm this, sedimentation experiments were conducted (Fig. S10), and the sedimentation rate constant (k) was calculated by exponential function fitting (Table S7). As shown in Fig. 7(b), the sedimentation rate constant of UCN with or without the addition of electrolyte (NaCl) was 9.2–26.8 times, and 1.7–3.88 times higher than that of TCN and MCN, respectively. After photoaging treatment, the aged UCN also exhibited higher sedimentation rate constant than aged TCN/MCN. This indeed confirmed that UCN had less water stability and more easily to be settled in aquatic environment. In addition, compared with fresh UCN, the aged UCN had smaller particle sizes (Table S3), which would result in even faster aggregation due to the higher surface energy of small particles [75]. In addition, the water stability of TCN was the highest, which indicated that TCN and its photo-transformation products might be widely spread with waterflow without sedimentation.

These results collectively indicated that during the environmental transportation of CNs, the photoaging process could play a significant role in determining their water stability, biological toxicity, and environmental fate. Notably, the CNs synthesized from different precursors had distinct reactivity under photoaging, which would lead to different environmental behaviors and effects if they were released to the aqueous environment. Such heterogeneity on environmental effects arising from different precursors of CNs needs to be considered when applying the CNs in large-scale engineering systems, including water disinfection and other environmental remediation applications.

In summary, this study elucidated the photo-transformation properties of CNs synthesized from 3 typical different precursors under photoaging treatment. All the CNs were essentially unstable under VL-induced photoaging, and the photo-stability followed the order of TCN < UCN < MCN. Physicochemical structural changes before and after photoaging were monitored, which demonstrated that no obvious crystal structural changes of CNs were found. More terminal amino groups were formed on TCN, which makes TCN more easily to be decomposed. Atomic ratio of O in TCN also increased after photoaging, suggesting TCN was more easily to be oxidized, in agreement with its lowest photo-stability among the CNs. ROSs including ·OH, ·O2 − and 1O2 were detected by ESR spin-trap technology, and H2O2 production efficiency was measured to follow the order of TCN > UCN > MCN. TCN could produce the highest amount of H2O2, due to its more negative CB potential than UCN and MCN. The order of produced H2O2 was consistent with the order of photo-stability of CNs, suggesting the non-radical species (i.e. H2O2) could play important roles in determining the photo-stability of CNs. A possible photo-transformation mechanisms involving the initial oxidation via H2O2, followed by ·OH/·O2 − ring-open oxidation was proposed. The photocatalytic disinfection performance was in the order of UCN > TCN > MCN, and the activity deterioration for UCN was more serious than that of TCN/MCN. It is unexpected to find that the CNs filtrates after photoaging possessed considerable photocatalytic bacterial inactivation activities, and TCN-FT showed the highest activity among the CN-FTs, which was due to the released carbon dots-like substances as confirmed by FT-IR and TEM observation. This can be explained by the lower oxidation ability of H2O2 led to the lower mineralization ability and more photo-transformation fragments. Sedimentation experiments showed UCN had the lowest water stability, which was easily settled in aquatic environment. While the water stability of TCN was the highest, suggesting TCN and its photo-transformation products might be widely spread with waterflow thus causing persistent adverse environmental effects. Overall, these results highlighted that the CNs synthesized from different precursors have distinct influence on photocatalytic water disinfection activity and environmental fate. Further investigations into decomposition pathways and by-products of different CNs, as well as their impacts on water ecologic environment, are required to assess the feasibility of large-scale applications. In addition, the exploration of more stable, safe, and eco-friendly CNs are imperative to bring CNs into practical applications with sustainable performances.
